items.text: How well does the organization define and document IT governance roles and responsibilities?

rubric.initial: IT governance roles are undefined or informal with no documented responsibilities.
rubric.developing: Some IT governance roles are defined but inconsistently documented and communicated.
rubric.established: IT governance roles and responsibilities are formally documented and communicated across the organization.
rubric.optimizing: IT governance roles are continuously refined based on organizational changes and industry best practices.

items.text: To what extent does a formal IT governance body (e.g., IT steering committee) exist and operate?

rubric.initial: No formal IT governance body exists; decisions are ad-hoc.
rubric.developing: A governance body exists on paper but meets irregularly with limited authority.
rubric.established: A governance body meets regularly with defined charter, membership, and decision authority.
rubric.optimizing: The governance body continuously adapts its scope and composition to address evolving strategic needs.

items.text: How effectively does the governance structure span across business units?

rubric.initial: Governance is siloed within IT with no cross-functional representation.
rubric.developing: Limited cross-functional participation exists but is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Governance includes formal cross-functional representation from key business units.
rubric.optimizing: Governance operates as a truly enterprise-wide function with embedded business participation and feedback loops.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT governance framework (e.g., COBIT, ISO 38500)?

rubric.initial: No recognized governance framework is adopted.
rubric.developing: Elements of a governance framework are used informally.
rubric.established: A recognized governance framework is formally adopted and implemented.
rubric.optimizing: The governance framework is benchmarked against peers and continuously improved.

items.text: How well does governance integrate with enterprise risk management?

rubric.initial: IT governance and enterprise risk management operate independently.
rubric.developing: Some linkage exists between IT governance and risk management but it is informal.
rubric.established: IT governance is formally integrated with enterprise risk management processes.
rubric.optimizing: Governance and risk management are tightly coupled with real-time risk visibility and adaptive controls.

items.text: To what degree are governance meeting outcomes documented and tracked?

rubric.initial: Governance meetings are undocumented with no action tracking.
rubric.developing: Minutes are occasionally taken but action items are not systematically tracked.
rubric.established: All governance meetings are documented with action items tracked to completion.
rubric.optimizing: Meeting outcomes drive measurable improvements and are linked to strategic KPIs.

items.text: How effectively does the governance structure handle escalation and exception management?

rubric.initial: No formal escalation or exception processes exist.
rubric.developing: Escalation paths exist but are unclear or inconsistently followed.
rubric.established: Formal escalation and exception management processes are defined and followed.
rubric.optimizing: Escalation processes include root-cause analysis and feed continuous improvement cycles.

items.text: How clearly are IT decision rights defined across the organization?

rubric.initial: Decision rights are undefined; authority is assumed or contested.
rubric.developing: Some decision rights are informally understood but not documented.
rubric.established: Decision rights are formally documented using a RACI or similar framework.
rubric.optimizing: Decision rights are regularly reviewed and optimized based on organizational performance data.

items.text: How well does the organization use decision frameworks (e.g., RACI) for IT investments?

rubric.initial: No decision frameworks are used for IT investments.
rubric.developing: Decision frameworks are partially used for major investments only.
rubric.established: Decision frameworks are consistently applied across all IT investment decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Decision frameworks are integrated into automated workflows with continuous refinement.

items.text: To what extent are IT investment decisions aligned with business strategy?

rubric.initial: IT investment decisions are made independently of business strategy.
rubric.developing: Some IT investments reference business strategy but alignment is inconsistent.
rubric.established: All IT investments are formally evaluated against business strategy criteria.
rubric.optimizing: IT investments are dynamically aligned with business strategy through real-time portfolio management.

items.text: How transparent is the decision-making process for IT priorities?

rubric.initial: IT prioritization is opaque with no visibility to stakeholders.
rubric.developing: Some decisions are communicated but the rationale is rarely shared.
rubric.established: Decision criteria and rationale are transparently communicated to stakeholders.
rubric.optimizing: Full transparency exists with stakeholder feedback loops influencing future decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization resolve conflicts in IT decision-making?

rubric.initial: Conflicts in IT decisions are unresolved or escalated informally.
rubric.developing: Conflict resolution exists but depends on individual relationships.
rubric.established: Formal conflict resolution mechanisms are defined and consistently used.
rubric.optimizing: Conflict resolution drives organizational learning and process improvement.

items.text: To what degree do business leaders participate in IT decision-making?

rubric.initial: Business leaders are not involved in IT decisions.
rubric.developing: Business leaders are consulted occasionally but not systematically.
rubric.established: Business leaders are formal participants in IT decision-making processes.
rubric.optimizing: Business and IT leaders jointly own decisions with shared accountability for outcomes.

items.text: How well does the organization track and evaluate the outcomes of IT decisions?

rubric.initial: Outcomes of IT decisions are not tracked.
rubric.developing: Some outcomes are reviewed but not systematically evaluated.
rubric.established: Decision outcomes are formally tracked and evaluated against expected benefits.
rubric.optimizing: Decision outcome analysis drives continuous improvement of decision-making processes.

items.text: How comprehensive is the organization's IT policy framework?

rubric.initial: No formal IT policies exist.
rubric.developing: Some IT policies exist but coverage is incomplete and inconsistent.
rubric.established: A comprehensive IT policy framework covers all major domains with regular reviews.
rubric.optimizing: The policy framework is continuously updated based on emerging risks, regulations, and best practices.

items.text: How effectively are IT policies communicated and enforced?

rubric.initial: IT policies are not communicated or enforced.
rubric.developing: Policies are published but enforcement is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Policies are actively communicated with defined enforcement mechanisms.
rubric.optimizing: Policy compliance is monitored in real-time with automated enforcement where possible.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the IT policy lifecycle (create, review, retire)?

rubric.initial: No policy lifecycle management exists.
rubric.developing: Policies are created but rarely reviewed or retired.
rubric.established: A formal policy lifecycle process includes scheduled reviews and retirement.
rubric.optimizing: Policy lifecycle management is automated with stakeholder feedback driving updates.

items.text: To what extent are IT policies aligned with regulatory and legal requirements?

rubric.initial: IT policies do not address regulatory requirements.
rubric.developing: Some policies reference regulations but alignment is incomplete.
rubric.established: All IT policies are mapped to applicable regulatory and legal requirements.
rubric.optimizing: Regulatory alignment is continuously monitored with proactive policy updates for new requirements.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage policy exceptions?

rubric.initial: Policy exceptions are not tracked or managed.
rubric.developing: Exceptions are handled ad-hoc without formal processes.
rubric.established: A formal exception management process includes documentation and approval workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Exception data is analyzed to identify systemic issues and drive policy improvements.

items.text: How well are IT policies integrated with organizational change management?

rubric.initial: Policy changes are not coordinated with change management.
rubric.developing: Some coordination exists but is informal.
rubric.established: Policy changes follow formal change management processes with impact assessment.
rubric.optimizing: Policy and change management are tightly integrated with automated impact analysis.

items.text: How mature is the organization's approach to policy awareness and training?

rubric.initial: No policy awareness or training programs exist.
rubric.developing: Basic policy training is provided during onboarding only.
rubric.established: Ongoing policy awareness programs include regular training and assessments.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive training programs use analytics to target policy knowledge gaps.

items.text: How well does the CIO/IT leadership align with C-suite strategic priorities?

rubric.initial: IT leadership operates in isolation from C-suite priorities.
rubric.developing: Periodic alignment discussions occur but are not structured.
rubric.established: IT leadership regularly participates in C-suite strategic planning with defined inputs.
rubric.optimizing: IT leadership co-creates strategy with the C-suite and is measured on business outcomes.

items.text: To what extent does the board or executive team receive IT performance reporting?

rubric.initial: No IT performance reporting reaches executive leadership.
rubric.developing: Ad-hoc IT reports are provided when requested.
rubric.established: Regular IT performance dashboards are delivered to executive leadership.
rubric.optimizing: Executive dashboards are interactive with real-time data driving strategic discussions.

items.text: How effectively does IT leadership communicate the value of technology investments?

rubric.initial: IT value communication is absent or purely technical.
rubric.developing: Some value communication exists but lacks business context.
rubric.established: IT value is communicated in business terms with clear ROI metrics.
rubric.optimizing: Value communication is continuous with real-time business impact visibility.

items.text: How well does executive alignment drive IT budget and resource allocation?

rubric.initial: IT budgets are set without executive strategic input.
rubric.developing: Executive input is sought but rarely influences IT budget decisions.
rubric.established: IT budgets are collaboratively developed with executive alignment to strategic priorities.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic budget allocation adjusts in real-time based on executive-aligned strategic shifts.

items.text: To what degree is there shared accountability between IT and business executives for outcomes?

rubric.initial: No shared accountability exists between IT and business.
rubric.developing: Informal expectations exist but accountability is unclear.
rubric.established: Formal shared accountability mechanisms are defined with joint KPIs.
rubric.optimizing: Shared accountability drives joint decision-making and continuous performance improvement.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage the IT-business relationship at the executive level?

rubric.initial: The IT-business relationship is adversarial or disconnected at the executive level.
rubric.developing: Relationships exist but are transactional and project-focused.
rubric.established: Strategic partnership is established with regular joint planning sessions.
rubric.optimizing: The IT-business relationship is a strategic differentiator with co-innovation.

items.text: How well does executive alignment support digital transformation initiatives?

rubric.initial: No executive alignment exists for digital transformation.
rubric.developing: Executives acknowledge digital transformation but provide limited support.
rubric.established: Executive sponsorship actively supports digital transformation with resources and mandate.
rubric.optimizing: Executives champion digital transformation with organization-wide cultural commitment.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT performance measurement framework?

rubric.initial: No IT performance metrics are defined or tracked.
rubric.developing: Basic metrics exist but are inconsistently collected and reported.
rubric.established: A comprehensive performance framework with KPIs is implemented and regularly reviewed.
rubric.optimizing: Performance measurement drives predictive analytics and proactive improvement.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use IT balanced scorecards or similar tools?

rubric.initial: No balanced scorecard or equivalent tool is used.
rubric.developing: A scorecard exists but is incomplete or rarely updated.
rubric.established: A balanced scorecard is actively maintained with regular stakeholder review.
rubric.optimizing: Scorecards are dynamic, integrated with real-time data, and drive strategic adjustments.

items.text: How well does the organization benchmark IT performance against industry peers?

rubric.initial: No benchmarking is performed.
rubric.developing: Occasional benchmarking occurs but is not systematic.
rubric.established: Regular benchmarking against industry peers informs improvement initiatives.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous benchmarking drives competitive positioning and best-practice adoption.

items.text: To what extent are IT performance metrics tied to business outcomes?

rubric.initial: IT metrics are purely technical with no business linkage.
rubric.developing: Some metrics have loose business connections.
rubric.established: IT metrics are formally mapped to business outcomes with clear causal links.
rubric.optimizing: Business outcome metrics drive IT performance targets in a closed-loop system.

items.text: How effectively does the organization act on IT performance data?

rubric.initial: Performance data is collected but not acted upon.
rubric.developing: Some actions result from performance reviews but follow-through is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Performance data consistently drives improvement actions with accountability.
rubric.optimizing: Automated performance triggers initiate corrective actions and continuous optimization.

items.text: How mature is the organization's approach to IT financial performance management?

rubric.initial: IT financial performance is not tracked beyond budget vs. actual.
rubric.developing: Basic financial metrics exist but lack depth (e.g., no TCO analysis).
rubric.established: Comprehensive IT financial management includes TCO, unit costs, and value metrics.
rubric.optimizing: Financial performance management enables dynamic resource optimization and investment rebalancing.

items.text: How well does the organization measure and report on IT project delivery performance?

rubric.initial: Project delivery performance is not measured.
rubric.developing: Basic project metrics (on-time, on-budget) are tracked inconsistently.
rubric.established: Project delivery performance is systematically measured with stage-gate reviews.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive project analytics enable proactive intervention and portfolio optimization.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT risk identification and assessment process?

rubric.initial: IT risks are not systematically identified or assessed.
rubric.developing: Some risks are identified but assessment is informal and incomplete.
rubric.established: A formal IT risk assessment process identifies, evaluates, and prioritizes risks regularly.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous risk sensing uses advanced analytics to identify emerging risks proactively.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain and use an IT risk register?

rubric.initial: No IT risk register exists.
rubric.developing: A risk register exists but is outdated or incomplete.
rubric.established: The risk register is actively maintained with assigned owners and mitigation plans.
rubric.optimizing: The risk register is dynamic, integrated with enterprise risk management, and drives real-time decisions.

items.text: How well does IT risk governance integrate with business continuity planning?

rubric.initial: IT risk governance and business continuity are disconnected.
rubric.developing: Some linkage exists but coordination is informal.
rubric.established: IT risk governance formally feeds business continuity planning processes.
rubric.optimizing: Integrated risk and continuity management enables rapid adaptive response to disruptions.

items.text: To what extent does the organization quantify IT risk in financial terms?

rubric.initial: IT risks are not quantified financially.
rubric.developing: Some high-profile risks have rough financial estimates.
rubric.established: IT risks are systematically quantified using established methodologies (e.g., FAIR).
rubric.optimizing: Financial risk quantification drives investment decisions and insurance strategies.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage third-party IT risks?

rubric.initial: Third-party IT risks are not assessed or managed.
rubric.developing: Key vendors are informally assessed but third-party risk management is incomplete.
rubric.established: A formal third-party risk management program covers all critical vendors.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous third-party risk monitoring uses automated tools and contractual controls.

items.text: How well does the organization's risk appetite statement address IT-specific risks?

rubric.initial: No risk appetite statement exists or it does not address IT.
rubric.developing: A general risk appetite exists but IT-specific thresholds are undefined.
rubric.established: IT-specific risk appetite and tolerance levels are defined and communicated.
rubric.optimizing: IT risk appetite dynamically adjusts based on threat landscape and business context.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT risk reporting to leadership?

rubric.initial: IT risk is not reported to leadership.
rubric.developing: Ad-hoc risk reports are provided when incidents occur.
rubric.established: Regular IT risk reports are delivered to leadership with trend analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time risk dashboards enable leadership to make informed strategic decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track regulatory compliance obligations related to IT?

rubric.initial: Regulatory compliance obligations are not tracked.
rubric.developing: Some obligations are known but tracking is manual and incomplete.
rubric.established: All regulatory obligations are cataloged with assigned owners and compliance tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Automated compliance tracking provides real-time status and proactive alerts for changes.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT audit management process?

rubric.initial: IT audits are reactive with no structured process.
rubric.developing: Audit responses are managed but preparation is inconsistent.
rubric.established: A formal audit management process includes preparation, response, and remediation tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous audit readiness is maintained with automated evidence collection.

items.text: How well does the organization manage IT compliance across multiple regulatory frameworks?

rubric.initial: Compliance is managed in silos for each regulation.
rubric.developing: Some consolidation exists but framework mapping is incomplete.
rubric.established: A unified compliance framework maps controls across multiple regulations.
rubric.optimizing: Automated cross-framework compliance management reduces duplication and ensures coverage.

items.text: To what extent does the organization automate compliance monitoring and reporting?

rubric.initial: No automation exists for compliance activities.
rubric.developing: Some compliance tasks are partially automated.
rubric.established: Key compliance monitoring and reporting activities are automated.
rubric.optimizing: End-to-end compliance automation includes continuous monitoring, reporting, and remediation.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage compliance evidence and documentation?

rubric.initial: Compliance evidence is scattered or unavailable.
rubric.developing: Evidence is collected but organization and retrieval are difficult.
rubric.established: A centralized compliance evidence repository is maintained with clear organization.
rubric.optimizing: Automated evidence collection and management ensures audit-ready documentation at all times.

items.text: How well does the organization address data privacy compliance (e.g., GDPR, CCPA)?

rubric.initial: Data privacy compliance is not addressed.
rubric.developing: Basic privacy measures exist but compliance is incomplete.
rubric.established: A comprehensive data privacy program addresses applicable regulations.
rubric.optimizing: Privacy-by-design principles are embedded with automated compliance verification.

items.text: How mature is the organization's approach to compliance training and awareness?

rubric.initial: No compliance training exists.
rubric.developing: Basic compliance training is provided annually.
rubric.established: Role-based compliance training is delivered with effectiveness measurement.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive compliance training uses analytics to target knowledge gaps and emerging requirements.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT strategic planning process?

rubric.initial: No formal IT strategic planning process exists.
rubric.developing: Strategic planning occurs sporadically without a defined methodology.
rubric.established: A formal strategic planning process is executed annually with defined methodology and stakeholder input.
rubric.optimizing: Strategic planning is a continuous process with real-time environmental scanning and adaptive adjustments.

items.text: How well does the IT strategy articulate a clear vision and mission?

rubric.initial: No IT vision or mission statement exists.
rubric.developing: An IT vision exists but is vague or disconnected from organizational mission.
rubric.established: A clear IT vision and mission are documented, communicated, and aligned with organizational goals.
rubric.optimizing: The IT vision and mission are living documents that inspire action and adapt to market dynamics.

items.text: To what extent does the IT strategy incorporate environmental scanning (PESTEL, SWOT)?

rubric.initial: No environmental analysis is performed.
rubric.developing: Occasional analysis is done informally.
rubric.established: Formal environmental scanning methodologies are applied during strategic planning.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous environmental sensing feeds real-time strategic adjustments.

items.text: How effectively does the organization translate IT strategy into actionable objectives?

rubric.initial: IT strategy is not translated into actionable objectives.
rubric.developing: Some objectives are defined but lack measurable targets.
rubric.established: IT strategy is decomposed into SMART objectives with assigned ownership.
rubric.optimizing: Strategic objectives cascade into operational plans with automated progress tracking.

items.text: How well does the organization conduct scenario planning for IT strategy?

rubric.initial: No scenario planning is performed.
rubric.developing: Informal what-if discussions occur but are not structured.
rubric.established: Formal scenario planning exercises evaluate multiple futures and inform strategy.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced scenario modeling uses simulation and data analytics for strategic resilience.

items.text: To what degree does the IT strategy address a multi-year horizon (3-5 years)?

rubric.initial: IT planning is limited to the current fiscal year.
rubric.developing: Some multi-year thinking exists but is not formalized.
rubric.established: A formal 3-5 year IT strategy is documented with milestone checkpoints.
rubric.optimizing: Long-term strategy is dynamically updated with rolling forecasts and trigger-based reviews.

items.text: How effectively does the organization review and refresh the IT strategy?

rubric.initial: IT strategy is never reviewed once created.
rubric.developing: Periodic reviews occur but are inconsistent.
rubric.established: Annual strategy reviews are conducted with defined criteria for refresh.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous strategy review uses performance data and market signals for real-time adaptation.

items.text: How well does IT strategy align with overall business strategy?

rubric.initial: IT strategy is developed independently of business strategy.
rubric.developing: Some alignment exists but it is informal and opportunistic.
rubric.established: IT strategy is formally derived from and mapped to business strategy.
rubric.optimizing: IT and business strategy are co-developed with full bidirectional alignment.

items.text: To what extent do business unit strategies incorporate IT capabilities?

rubric.initial: Business units do not consider IT capabilities in their planning.
rubric.developing: Some business units consider IT but integration is inconsistent.
rubric.established: All business unit strategies formally incorporate IT capability assessments.
rubric.optimizing: IT capabilities are a primary driver of business unit strategy and competitive advantage.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use business capability mapping to align IT?

rubric.initial: No business capability mapping exists.
rubric.developing: Basic capability mapping exists but is not used for IT alignment.
rubric.established: Business capability maps are used to align IT investments and priorities.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic capability mapping drives real-time IT resource allocation and investment decisions.

items.text: How well does the organization measure business-IT alignment?

rubric.initial: Business-IT alignment is not measured.
rubric.developing: Alignment is informally assessed through anecdotal feedback.
rubric.established: Formal alignment metrics are tracked and reported to leadership.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous alignment measurement drives proactive adjustments to strategy and execution.

items.text: To what degree do IT and business leaders share a common understanding of strategic priorities?

rubric.initial: IT and business leaders have divergent views on priorities.
rubric.developing: Some shared understanding exists but gaps are common.
rubric.established: Regular joint planning ensures a shared understanding of priorities.
rubric.optimizing: Unified strategic planning creates seamless alignment with shared accountability.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage the demand-supply balance for IT services?

rubric.initial: IT demand and supply are not managed cohesively.
rubric.developing: Basic demand management exists but supply planning is reactive.
rubric.established: Formal demand-supply balancing processes align IT capacity with business needs.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive demand analytics and elastic supply models optimize resource utilization.

items.text: How well does the organization align IT operating models to business needs?

rubric.initial: No defined IT operating model exists.
rubric.developing: A basic operating model exists but does not reflect business needs.
rubric.established: The IT operating model is designed to support business requirements and is regularly reviewed.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive IT operating models dynamically evolve to match changing business contexts.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT roadmap development process?

rubric.initial: No IT roadmap exists.
rubric.developing: Roadmaps are created ad-hoc for specific projects only.
rubric.established: A comprehensive IT roadmap is maintained with defined milestones and dependencies.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic roadmaps are continuously updated based on strategic shifts and delivery progress.

items.text: How well does the IT roadmap integrate with enterprise architecture plans?

rubric.initial: No integration between roadmap and architecture plans.
rubric.developing: Some reference to architecture exists but integration is weak.
rubric.established: The IT roadmap is formally aligned with enterprise architecture target states.
rubric.optimizing: Roadmap and architecture are tightly coupled with automated consistency checks.

items.text: To what extent does the roadmap reflect cross-functional dependencies?

rubric.initial: Cross-functional dependencies are not identified in the roadmap.
rubric.developing: Some dependencies are noted but management is informal.
rubric.established: Cross-functional dependencies are mapped, managed, and communicated through the roadmap.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced dependency analytics optimize sequencing and identify risks proactively.

items.text: How effectively does the organization communicate the IT roadmap to stakeholders?

rubric.initial: The IT roadmap is not communicated to stakeholders.
rubric.developing: Roadmap is shared occasionally but not updated for audience relevance.
rubric.established: Roadmap communication is tailored by audience with regular updates.
rubric.optimizing: Interactive roadmap portals provide real-time visibility to all stakeholders.

items.text: How well does the roadmap balance short-term needs with long-term strategic goals?

rubric.initial: Roadmap is dominated by short-term tactical needs.
rubric.developing: Some long-term items appear but are frequently deprioritized.
rubric.established: The roadmap formally balances short-term and long-term initiatives with clear rationale.
rubric.optimizing: Portfolio optimization algorithms dynamically balance time horizons based on value and risk.

items.text: To what degree does the roadmap include technology refresh and lifecycle management?

rubric.initial: Technology lifecycle is not addressed in the roadmap.
rubric.developing: Some technology refresh items are included reactively.
rubric.established: Technology lifecycle management is systematically included in roadmap planning.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive lifecycle analytics drive proactive technology refresh planning.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track roadmap execution and adjust plans?

rubric.initial: Roadmap execution is not tracked.
rubric.developing: Basic progress tracking exists but adjustments are rare.
rubric.established: Roadmap execution is formally tracked with regular review and adjustment cycles.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time execution tracking with automated variance alerts enables agile plan adjustments.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT portfolio prioritization process?

rubric.initial: No formal prioritization process exists for IT investments.
rubric.developing: Prioritization occurs but criteria are inconsistent or subjective.
rubric.established: A formal prioritization framework with defined criteria is consistently applied.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic prioritization uses real-time data, optimization models, and strategic alignment scoring.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use scoring models or criteria for IT prioritization?

rubric.initial: No scoring criteria are used.
rubric.developing: Informal criteria are applied inconsistently.
rubric.established: Standardized scoring models evaluate all IT initiatives against defined criteria.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced multi-criteria decision analysis with sensitivity testing optimizes portfolio selection.

items.text: To what extent does the prioritization process consider risk-adjusted returns?

rubric.initial: Risk is not considered in prioritization.
rubric.developing: Risk is qualitatively discussed but not systematically factored.
rubric.established: Risk-adjusted return analysis is a formal component of prioritization.
rubric.optimizing: Monte Carlo simulation and advanced risk modeling optimize risk-return trade-offs.

items.text: How well does the organization balance the IT portfolio across run, grow, and transform?

rubric.initial: No portfolio balance framework exists.
rubric.developing: Balance is discussed informally but not measured or managed.
rubric.established: Portfolio balance targets are defined and tracked across investment categories.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic portfolio rebalancing optimizes investment mix based on strategic objectives and market conditions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage the IT project pipeline?

rubric.initial: No pipeline management exists; projects start without queue management.
rubric.developing: A basic project queue exists but intake is not controlled.
rubric.established: Formal pipeline management governs project intake, prioritization, and resource allocation.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive pipeline analytics optimize throughput and resource utilization across the portfolio.

items.text: To what degree does the prioritization process engage stakeholders from across the organization?

rubric.initial: Prioritization is done by IT alone.
rubric.developing: Some business input is gathered but not systematically.
rubric.established: Cross-functional stakeholder input is a formal part of the prioritization process.
rubric.optimizing: Stakeholders are empowered with transparent tools to participate in real-time prioritization.

items.text: How well does the organization deprioritize or stop underperforming IT initiatives?

rubric.initial: Underperforming initiatives are rarely stopped.
rubric.developing: Some initiatives are paused but formal kill criteria are lacking.
rubric.established: Defined kill criteria and stage-gate reviews enable timely deprioritization.
rubric.optimizing: Portfolio performance analytics proactively flag initiatives for review or termination.

items.text: How effectively does the organization define expected value from IT investments?

rubric.initial: Expected value is not defined for IT investments.
rubric.developing: Informal value expectations exist but are not documented.
rubric.established: Formal business cases define expected value with measurable benefits.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced value modeling quantifies tangible and intangible benefits with confidence intervals.

items.text: How well does the organization track benefits realization after IT project delivery?

rubric.initial: Benefits realization is not tracked post-delivery.
rubric.developing: Some post-delivery reviews occur but tracking is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Formal benefits realization tracking compares actual to projected value.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous benefits monitoring with automated dashboards drives accountability and learning.

items.text: To what extent does the organization have a benefits realization management framework?

rubric.initial: No benefits realization framework exists.
rubric.developing: Informal processes exist for high-profile projects only.
rubric.established: A formal benefits realization management framework is applied to all significant investments.
rubric.optimizing: An enterprise-wide benefits management office orchestrates value realization with predictive analytics.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure IT's contribution to business value?

rubric.initial: IT's business value contribution is not measured.
rubric.developing: Value is anecdotally reported but not systematically measured.
rubric.established: IT value contribution is measured through defined metrics linked to business outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time value tracking demonstrates IT's strategic impact with causal attribution models.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the transition from project delivery to value realization?

rubric.initial: No formal transition process exists; projects end at go-live.
rubric.developing: Some handoff occurs but operational readiness is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Formal transition management ensures operational readiness and benefits ownership transfer.
rubric.optimizing: Seamless transition with embedded value coaches ensures sustained benefit delivery.

items.text: To what degree does the organization use post-implementation reviews to capture lessons?

rubric.initial: Post-implementation reviews are not conducted.
rubric.developing: Reviews occur occasionally but lessons are not systematically captured.
rubric.established: Formal PIRs are conducted with lessons documented and shared.
rubric.optimizing: PIR insights are systematically integrated into planning processes and organizational knowledge bases.

items.text: How effectively does the organization communicate realized IT value to stakeholders?

rubric.initial: Realized value is not communicated.
rubric.developing: Value is occasionally reported in project closeout documents.
rubric.established: Regular value realization reports are delivered to stakeholders with trend analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Interactive value dashboards provide real-time visibility to all stakeholders.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and map IT stakeholders?

rubric.initial: IT stakeholders are not identified or mapped.
rubric.developing: Key stakeholders are informally known but not systematically mapped.
rubric.established: A formal stakeholder map is maintained with influence and interest analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic stakeholder mapping adapts to organizational changes and initiative contexts.

items.text: How well does the organization manage stakeholder expectations for IT delivery?

rubric.initial: Stakeholder expectations are not managed.
rubric.developing: Expectations are discussed informally on a project-by-project basis.
rubric.established: Formal expectation management processes are embedded in project and service delivery.
rubric.optimizing: Proactive expectation management uses predictive analytics and sentiment analysis.

rubric.optimizing: Advanced analytics with machine learning optimize staffing, routing, and resolution strategies.

items.text: How well does the service desk manage knowledge for support staff?

rubric.initial: No knowledge management exists for support staff.
rubric.developing: Some documentation is available but not structured.
rubric.established: A formal knowledge management system supports consistent issue resolution.
rubric.optimizing: AI-curated knowledge with contextual recommendations accelerates resolution and onboarding.

items.text: How effectively does IT measure end-user satisfaction with IT services?

rubric.initial: End-user satisfaction is not measured.
rubric.developing: Occasional informal feedback is collected.
rubric.established: Regular satisfaction surveys are conducted with trend analysis and action planning.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous experience monitoring with real-time feedback drives immediate service improvements.

items.text: How well does the organization use Net Promoter Score or similar metrics for IT?

rubric.initial: No NPS or similar metrics are used for IT.
rubric.developing: Basic satisfaction ratings are collected but NPS methodology is not used.
rubric.established: NPS or equivalent is systematically measured and benchmarked.
rubric.optimizing: NPS drives strategic service decisions with segmented analysis and predictive modeling.

items.text: To what extent does IT act on customer satisfaction feedback?

rubric.initial: Feedback is not acted upon.
rubric.developing: Some feedback drives ad-hoc improvements.
rubric.established: Formal processes translate feedback into prioritized improvement actions.
rubric.optimizing: Closed-loop feedback management with automated action tracking drives continuous satisfaction improvement.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure satisfaction across different service channels?

rubric.initial: Channel-specific satisfaction is not measured.
rubric.developing: Some channels are measured inconsistently.
rubric.established: Satisfaction is measured across all service channels with comparative analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Cross-channel satisfaction analytics optimize channel strategy and resource allocation.

items.text: How well does IT understand the end-to-end customer journey?

rubric.initial: No customer journey mapping exists for IT services.
rubric.developing: Some touchpoints are understood but the full journey is not mapped.
rubric.established: Customer journeys are mapped for key IT services with pain point identification.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic journey analytics with experience scoring drive proactive experience optimization.

items.text: To what degree does the organization benchmark IT satisfaction against peers?

rubric.initial: No benchmarking of IT satisfaction occurs.
rubric.developing: Informal awareness of peer satisfaction levels exists.
rubric.established: Formal benchmarking compares IT satisfaction against industry peers.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous benchmarking with competitive analysis drives strategic service positioning.

items.text: How effectively does the organization address IT service experience gaps?

rubric.initial: Service experience gaps are not identified.
rubric.developing: Gaps are identified but remediation is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Formal gap analysis drives prioritized experience improvement programs.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive experience analytics identify emerging gaps before they impact satisfaction.

items.text: How mature is the organization's continual service improvement (CSI) process?

rubric.initial: No CSI process exists.
rubric.developing: Ad-hoc improvements occur but are not systematic.
rubric.established: A formal CSI program with defined methodology operates across IT services.
rubric.optimizing: Data-driven CSI with automated improvement identification and tracking is embedded in culture.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and prioritize improvement opportunities?

rubric.initial: Improvement opportunities are not systematically identified.
rubric.developing: Some opportunities are identified reactively.
rubric.established: Formal assessment identifies and prioritizes improvement opportunities with business impact analysis.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered analysis proactively identifies high-value improvement opportunities across the service portfolio.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track improvement initiative outcomes?

rubric.initial: Improvement outcomes are not tracked.
rubric.developing: Some outcomes are anecdotally reported.
rubric.established: Improvement outcomes are formally measured against defined success criteria.
rubric.optimizing: Automated outcome tracking with value realization reporting drives accountability and learning.

items.text: How well does the organization use service management maturity assessments?

rubric.initial: No maturity assessments are conducted.
rubric.developing: Informal self-assessments occur occasionally.
rubric.established: Regular maturity assessments against recognized frameworks (e.g., ITIL) drive improvement.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous maturity monitoring with automated assessment and benchmarking drives excellence.

items.text: How effectively does the organization learn from service failures?

rubric.initial: No learning from service failures occurs.
rubric.developing: Post-mortems are conducted inconsistently.
rubric.established: Formal blameless post-mortems drive systemic improvements.
rubric.optimizing: Learning from failure is embedded in culture with automated pattern detection across incidents.

items.text: To what degree does the organization share improvement best practices across teams?

rubric.initial: No best practice sharing occurs.
rubric.developing: Informal sharing happens between individuals.
rubric.established: Formal channels share improvement best practices across IT teams.
rubric.optimizing: Community-driven improvement platforms with recognition drive continuous knowledge sharing.

items.text: How well does the organization integrate customer feedback into service improvement?

rubric.initial: Customer feedback does not inform service improvement.
rubric.developing: Some feedback is considered informally.
rubric.established: Customer feedback is a formal input to the CSI prioritization process.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time customer feedback with sentiment analysis drives immediate and strategic improvements.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT vendor management process?

rubric.initial: No formal vendor management process exists.
rubric.developing: Basic vendor tracking exists but management is reactive.
rubric.established: Comprehensive vendor management includes evaluation, performance monitoring, and relationship management.
rubric.optimizing: Strategic vendor management drives innovation partnerships and optimized value from the vendor ecosystem.

items.text: How effectively does the organization evaluate and select IT vendors?

rubric.initial: Vendor selection is ad-hoc with no formal criteria.
rubric.developing: Some evaluation criteria exist but application is inconsistent.
rubric.established: A formal vendor evaluation framework is consistently applied with stakeholder input.
rubric.optimizing: Multi-criteria vendor evaluation with market intelligence and risk analysis optimizes selection.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage vendor performance against contracts?

rubric.initial: Vendor performance is not tracked.
rubric.developing: Some performance monitoring occurs for major vendors.
rubric.established: All significant vendor contracts have defined KPIs with regular performance reviews.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time vendor performance dashboards with automated compliance monitoring drive accountability.

items.text: How well does the organization manage vendor risk?

rubric.initial: Vendor risk is not assessed.
rubric.developing: Major vendor risks are informally monitored.
rubric.established: Formal vendor risk assessment covers financial, operational, and security risks.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous vendor risk monitoring with automated risk scoring and mitigation tracking.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage multi-vendor environments?

rubric.initial: Multi-vendor management is chaotic with finger-pointing.
rubric.developing: Some coordination exists but integration is poor.
rubric.established: Formal service integration governance manages multi-vendor delivery.
rubric.optimizing: SIAM practices optimize multi-vendor collaboration with end-to-end service accountability.

items.text: To what degree does the organization manage the vendor lifecycle?

rubric.initial: No vendor lifecycle management exists.
rubric.developing: Vendor onboarding is managed but lifecycle events are not.
rubric.established: Full vendor lifecycle management from selection through exit is formalized.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive vendor lifecycle management proactively manages transitions and renewals.

items.text: How well does the organization leverage strategic vendor relationships for innovation?

rubric.initial: Vendor relationships are purely transactional.
rubric.developing: Some vendors provide technology updates informally.
rubric.established: Strategic vendor partnerships include innovation roadmap sharing and co-development.
rubric.optimizing: Deep vendor ecosystems drive co-innovation with shared investment and mutual value creation.

items.text: How well-defined is the organization's cloud strategy?

rubric.initial: No cloud strategy exists.
rubric.developing: Cloud adoption is ad-hoc with no guiding strategy.
rubric.established: A formal cloud strategy defines adoption principles, target platforms, and migration priorities.
rubric.optimizing: Cloud strategy is continuously refined with multi-cloud optimization and FinOps practices.

items.text: To what extent has the organization adopted a cloud-first or cloud-smart policy?

rubric.initial: No cloud adoption policy exists.
rubric.developing: Informal cloud preference exists for some workloads.
rubric.established: A formal cloud-first/cloud-smart policy guides technology decisions with defined criteria.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic workload placement optimizes across cloud, hybrid, and on-premises based on real-time analysis.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage cloud financial operations (FinOps)?

rubric.initial: Cloud costs are not tracked or managed.
rubric.developing: Basic cost reporting exists but optimization is limited.
rubric.established: FinOps practices include budgeting, forecasting, and optimization with defined accountability.
rubric.optimizing: Mature FinOps with automated optimization, chargeback, and real-time cost anomaly detection.

items.text: How well does the cloud strategy address data sovereignty and compliance?

rubric.initial: Data sovereignty and compliance are not considered.
rubric.developing: Basic awareness exists but controls are incomplete.
rubric.established: Formal data residency and compliance requirements are embedded in cloud strategy.
rubric.optimizing: Automated compliance enforcement with continuous monitoring across all cloud environments.

items.text: To what degree does the organization manage multi-cloud or hybrid-cloud effectively?

rubric.initial: No multi-cloud or hybrid strategy exists.
rubric.developing: Multiple clouds are used but management is fragmented.
rubric.established: A coherent multi-cloud/hybrid strategy with common tooling is implemented.
rubric.optimizing: Unified multi-cloud management with workload portability and automated optimization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage cloud governance?

rubric.initial: No cloud governance exists.
rubric.developing: Basic guardrails exist but enforcement is inconsistent.
rubric.established: Comprehensive cloud governance includes landing zones, policies, and automated controls.
rubric.optimizing: Self-service cloud governance with policy-as-code enables speed with compliance.

items.text: How well does the organization develop cloud-native skills and capabilities?

rubric.initial: No cloud skills development program exists.
rubric.developing: Some individuals pursue cloud certifications independently.
rubric.established: A formal cloud skills program with certification paths and hands-on training is implemented.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous cloud capability development with innovation labs and community-driven learning.

items.text: How effectively does the organization assess and plan infrastructure modernization?

rubric.initial: No infrastructure modernization planning exists.
rubric.developing: Some legacy systems are identified but no modernization plan exists.
rubric.established: A formal modernization assessment identifies priorities with migration strategies.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous infrastructure assessment with automated modernization recommendations and ROI analysis.

items.text: To what extent has the organization reduced legacy infrastructure dependencies?

rubric.initial: Heavy reliance on legacy infrastructure with no reduction plan.
rubric.developing: Some legacy systems are being retired but progress is slow.
rubric.established: Active legacy reduction with defined milestones and application rationalization.
rubric.optimizing: Minimal legacy footprint with automated lifecycle management and proactive retirement.

items.text: How well does the organization use infrastructure-as-code (IaC)?

rubric.initial: No IaC practices exist; infrastructure is manually provisioned.
rubric.developing: Some IaC exists for select environments.
rubric.established: IaC is standard practice for all infrastructure provisioning with version control.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive IaC with drift detection, automated testing, and policy compliance.

items.text: How effectively does the organization implement network modernization?

rubric.initial: Network infrastructure relies on legacy designs with no modernization.
rubric.developing: Some network modernization is underway but inconsistent.
rubric.established: Software-defined networking and modern network architecture are implemented.
rubric.optimizing: Intent-based networking with automated optimization and zero-trust microsegmentation.

items.text: How well does the organization manage infrastructure automation?

rubric.initial: No infrastructure automation exists.
rubric.developing: Some automated tasks exist but automation is limited.
rubric.established: Comprehensive automation covers provisioning, configuration, and operations.
rubric.optimizing: Autonomous infrastructure operations with self-healing and predictive optimization.

items.text: To what degree has the organization adopted containerization and orchestration?

rubric.initial: No containerization exists.
rubric.developing: Some applications are containerized but orchestration is limited.
rubric.established: Container platforms with orchestration are used for modern application deployment.
rubric.optimizing: Enterprise container platform with automated scaling, service mesh, and developer self-service.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage technical debt in infrastructure?

rubric.initial: Technical debt is not tracked or managed.
rubric.developing: Some awareness of technical debt exists but no systematic approach.
rubric.established: Technical debt is inventoried, quantified, and addressed through planned reduction efforts.
rubric.optimizing: Automated technical debt detection with business impact analysis drives prioritized remediation.

items.text: How mature is the organization's platform engineering practice?

rubric.initial: No platform engineering practice exists.
rubric.developing: Some shared platforms exist but are not managed as products.
rubric.established: Platform engineering provides self-service internal platforms with defined SLAs.
rubric.optimizing: Mature platform engineering with internal developer platforms, golden paths, and developer experience focus.

items.text: How effectively does the organization provide developer self-service capabilities?

rubric.initial: No developer self-service exists.
rubric.developing: Basic self-service for some resources is available.
rubric.established: Comprehensive self-service portals enable developers to provision resources and deploy applications.
rubric.optimizing: Internal developer portals with automated guardrails enable full application lifecycle self-service.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage platforms as products?

rubric.initial: Platforms are not managed as products.
rubric.developing: Some platform teams exist but product thinking is absent.
rubric.established: Platform teams operate with product management practices including roadmaps and user feedback.
rubric.optimizing: Platform-as-a-product with dedicated teams, user research, and continuous value delivery.

items.text: How well does the organization standardize development environments and toolchains?

rubric.initial: No standardization of development environments exists.
rubric.developing: Some common tools are used but environments vary widely.
rubric.established: Standard development environments and toolchains are provided and supported.
rubric.optimizing: Automated development environment provisioning with pre-configured, optimized toolchains.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage API platforms and API lifecycle?

rubric.initial: No API management exists.
rubric.developing: APIs exist but management is ad-hoc.
rubric.established: An API management platform governs API lifecycle, security, and consumption.
rubric.optimizing: API-first strategy with developer portals, analytics, and monetization capabilities.

items.text: To what degree does the organization provide observability platforms?

rubric.initial: No centralized observability exists.
rubric.developing: Basic monitoring tools are used inconsistently.
rubric.established: Unified observability platform covers metrics, logs, and traces across services.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced observability with AIOps, automated root cause analysis, and business impact correlation.

items.text: How well does the organization manage data platforms and data infrastructure?

rubric.initial: No managed data platform exists.
rubric.developing: Basic data infrastructure supports some analytics.
rubric.established: Modern data platform supports data engineering, analytics, and machine learning workloads.
rubric.optimizing: Self-service data platform with automated governance, quality, and democratized access.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and catalog technical debt?

rubric.initial: Technical debt is not identified or tracked.
rubric.developing: Some technical debt is known informally.
rubric.established: Technical debt is systematically inventoried with impact assessment.
rubric.optimizing: Automated technical debt detection with real-time cataloging and business impact quantification.

items.text: How well does the organization quantify the cost of technical debt?

rubric.initial: Technical debt costs are not quantified.
rubric.developing: Rough estimates exist for major debt items.
rubric.established: Technical debt is quantified in terms of maintenance cost, risk, and opportunity cost.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive TCO models for technical debt inform investment decisions and strategic planning.

items.text: To what extent does the organization allocate resources for technical debt reduction?

rubric.initial: No resources are allocated for debt reduction.
rubric.developing: Occasional time is allocated but it is frequently reprioritized.
rubric.established: A defined percentage of capacity is allocated to technical debt reduction.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic resource allocation balances debt reduction with new development based on value optimization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization prevent new technical debt accumulation?

rubric.initial: No measures exist to prevent technical debt.
rubric.developing: Some coding standards exist but enforcement is weak.
rubric.established: Architecture reviews, coding standards, and quality gates prevent unnecessary debt.
rubric.optimizing: Automated quality enforcement with technical debt impact analysis prevents accumulation at the source.

items.text: How well does the organization prioritize technical debt remediation?

rubric.initial: Technical debt remediation is not prioritized.
rubric.developing: High-visibility issues are addressed reactively.
rubric.established: Formal prioritization framework balances debt remediation against business value and risk.
rubric.optimizing: Data-driven prioritization uses risk modeling and business impact to optimize remediation sequencing.

items.text: To what degree does technical debt management align with architecture governance?

rubric.initial: No alignment between technical debt and architecture governance.
rubric.developing: Architecture reviews sometimes identify debt but follow-through is weak.
rubric.established: Technical debt management is integrated with architecture governance and standards compliance.
rubric.optimizing: Unified architecture and debt governance with automated compliance and remediation tracking.

items.text: How effectively does the organization communicate technical debt to business stakeholders?

rubric.initial: Technical debt is not communicated to business stakeholders.
rubric.developing: Technical debt is mentioned in technical terms that business does not understand.
rubric.established: Technical debt is communicated in business terms with impact on delivery and risk.
rubric.optimizing: Visual technical debt dashboards with business impact projections inform strategic conversations.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT capacity planning process?

rubric.initial: No capacity planning exists.
rubric.developing: Reactive capacity management addresses issues after they occur.
rubric.established: Proactive capacity planning uses trend analysis and forecasting.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive capacity management with machine learning optimizes resource utilization automatically.

items.text: How effectively does the organization forecast IT resource demand?

rubric.initial: IT resource demand is not forecasted.
rubric.developing: Basic forecasting uses historical trends only.
rubric.established: Demand forecasting incorporates business growth projections and planned initiatives.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced demand modeling uses multiple data sources and machine learning for accurate forecasting.

items.text: To what extent does capacity planning cover all infrastructure components?

rubric.initial: Capacity planning covers only select components (e.g., storage).
rubric.developing: Key components are planned but coverage is incomplete.
rubric.established: Comprehensive capacity planning covers compute, storage, network, and cloud resources.
rubric.optimizing: End-to-end capacity planning includes application performance and business transaction capacity.

items.text: How well does the organization manage capacity in cloud environments?

rubric.initial: Cloud capacity is not managed; resources are over-provisioned.
rubric.developing: Basic cloud resource right-sizing is performed occasionally.
rubric.established: Cloud capacity management includes auto-scaling, right-sizing, and reservation planning.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent cloud capacity optimization with automated scaling and cost-performance balancing.

items.text: How effectively does the organization conduct capacity trending and reporting?

rubric.initial: No capacity trending or reporting exists.
rubric.developing: Basic reports show current utilization but no trends.
rubric.established: Regular capacity trending reports inform planning with threshold-based alerts.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time capacity dashboards with predictive trending and automated alerting.

items.text: To what degree does capacity planning inform financial planning?

rubric.initial: Capacity and financial planning are disconnected.
rubric.developing: Some cost implications of capacity are considered.
rubric.established: Capacity planning formally informs IT financial planning and budgeting.
rubric.optimizing: Integrated capacity-financial models enable dynamic cost optimization and investment planning.

items.text: How well does the organization manage performance capacity for applications?

rubric.initial: Application performance capacity is not managed.
rubric.developing: Performance testing occurs before deployment but ongoing management is lacking.
rubric.established: Application performance capacity is monitored and managed with defined thresholds.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous application performance management with predictive analytics and automated optimization.

items.text: How mature is the organization's disaster recovery program?

rubric.initial: No disaster recovery program exists.
rubric.developing: Basic backup procedures exist but DR planning is incomplete.
rubric.established: A comprehensive DR program with documented plans and defined RTOs/RPOs is in place.
rubric.optimizing: DR program is continuously tested and optimized with automated failover and recovery.

items.text: How effectively does the organization define and maintain RTOs and RPOs?

rubric.initial: RTOs and RPOs are not defined.
rubric.developing: Some critical systems have informal recovery targets.
rubric.established: RTOs and RPOs are formally defined based on business impact analysis for all critical systems.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic RTOs/RPOs adjust based on business context with automated validation against capabilities.

items.text: To what extent does the organization test disaster recovery plans?

rubric.initial: DR plans are never tested.
rubric.developing: Occasional tabletop exercises are conducted.
rubric.established: Regular DR tests including full failover exercises are conducted with documented results.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous DR testing with automated chaos engineering validates recovery capabilities.

items.text: How well does DR planning address cloud and hybrid environments?

rubric.initial: DR planning does not address cloud environments.
rubric.developing: Basic cloud backup exists but DR for cloud is incomplete.
rubric.established: Cloud DR strategies leverage native cloud resilience features with tested procedures.
rubric.optimizing: Multi-region, multi-cloud DR with automated failover and continuous recovery validation.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain DR documentation?

rubric.initial: No DR documentation exists.
rubric.developing: Basic documentation exists but is outdated.
rubric.established: Comprehensive DR documentation is maintained and regularly updated.
rubric.optimizing: Living DR documentation with automated updates and role-based runbooks.

items.text: To what degree does DR planning integrate with business continuity management?

rubric.initial: DR and business continuity are disconnected.
rubric.developing: Some coordination exists for critical systems.
rubric.established: DR is formally integrated with business continuity planning and tested together.
rubric.optimizing: Unified resilience management ensures end-to-end business continuity with automated orchestration.

items.text: How well does the organization manage data backup and recovery?

rubric.initial: No formal backup strategy exists.
rubric.developing: Basic backups are performed but recovery testing is rare.
rubric.established: Comprehensive backup strategy with regular recovery testing and immutable backups.
rubric.optimizing: Automated backup management with continuous recovery validation and ransomware-resilient architecture.

items.text: How mature is the organization's DevOps adoption?

rubric.initial: No DevOps practices exist; development and operations are siloed.
rubric.developing: Some DevOps tools are used but cultural adoption is limited.
rubric.established: DevOps practices are broadly adopted with CI/CD, collaboration, and shared responsibility.
rubric.optimizing: DevOps culture is fully embedded with advanced practices including GitOps, SRE, and platform engineering.

items.text: How effectively does the organization implement CI/CD pipelines?

rubric.initial: No CI/CD pipelines exist.
rubric.developing: Basic CI exists but CD is limited to select applications.
rubric.established: CI/CD pipelines are standard for application delivery with automated testing.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced CI/CD with progressive delivery, canary deployments, and automated rollback.

items.text: To what extent does the organization practice infrastructure-as-code in DevOps?

rubric.initial: IaC is not used in DevOps practices.
rubric.developing: Some IaC exists but is not integrated into delivery pipelines.
rubric.established: IaC is integrated into CI/CD pipelines for consistent infrastructure deployment.
rubric.optimizing: Full GitOps with declarative infrastructure, automated drift detection, and self-healing.

items.text: How well does the organization implement automated testing in delivery pipelines?

rubric.initial: No automated testing exists in pipelines.
rubric.developing: Basic unit tests are automated but coverage is low.
rubric.established: Comprehensive automated testing includes unit, integration, and end-to-end tests.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent test automation with AI-assisted test generation, optimization, and continuous quality validation.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure DevOps performance (DORA metrics)?

rubric.initial: DevOps performance is not measured.
rubric.developing: Some metrics are tracked but not systematically.
rubric.established: DORA metrics (deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR, change failure rate) are tracked and targeted.
rubric.optimizing: DORA metrics drive continuous improvement with automated insights and team-level optimization.

items.text: To what degree does the organization implement security in DevOps (DevSecOps)?

rubric.initial: Security is not integrated into DevOps.
rubric.developing: Some security checks occur but are not automated.
rubric.established: Security is embedded in CI/CD pipelines with automated scanning and policy enforcement.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive DevSecOps with shift-left security, automated compliance, and continuous threat modeling.

items.text: How well does the organization manage release and deployment processes?

rubric.initial: Release management is ad-hoc and error-prone.
rubric.developing: Basic release processes exist but are largely manual.
rubric.established: Formal release management with automated deployment and rollback procedures.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced release orchestration with feature flags, progressive rollout, and automated quality gates.

pillars.name: Workforce & Culture
pillars.togaf_ref: TOGAF 10 — Architecture Skills Framework, Organization & Governance

items.text: How effectively does the organization assess current IT workforce skills?

rubric.initial: No IT skills assessment is performed.
rubric.developing: Informal skill awareness exists but no systematic assessment.
rubric.established: Regular skills assessments identify gaps with competency frameworks.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous skills analytics with market benchmarking drive strategic workforce planning.

items.text: How well does the organization maintain an IT skills inventory?

rubric.initial: No skills inventory exists.
rubric.developing: Basic role descriptions exist but individual skills are not tracked.
rubric.established: A comprehensive skills inventory is maintained for all IT staff.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic skills platform with self-assessment, peer validation, and automated gap analysis.

items.text: To what extent does skills assessment inform training and development plans?

rubric.initial: Skills assessment does not inform training.
rubric.developing: Some training is aligned to known gaps informally.
rubric.established: Skills gaps directly drive individual and team development plans.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven learning recommendations personalize development based on skills data and career paths.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify future skill requirements?

rubric.initial: Future skill requirements are not anticipated.
rubric.developing: Some awareness of emerging skill needs exists.
rubric.established: Formal analysis identifies future skill requirements aligned with technology strategy.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive workforce analytics with market intelligence drive proactive skill development.

items.text: How well does the organization assess soft skills and leadership capabilities in IT?

rubric.initial: Only technical skills are considered.
rubric.developing: Soft skills are informally observed but not assessed.
rubric.established: Formal assessment of soft skills and leadership capabilities is part of development planning.
rubric.optimizing: Holistic competency assessment integrates technical, soft, and leadership skills with 360 feedback.

items.text: To what degree does the organization benchmark IT skills against industry standards?

rubric.initial: No benchmarking of IT skills occurs.
rubric.developing: Informal comparison with industry expectations exists.
rubric.established: IT skills are benchmarked against recognized competency frameworks and certifications.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous market benchmarking drives competitive compensation and development strategies.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track skills development progress?

rubric.initial: Skills development progress is not tracked.
rubric.developing: Completion of training is tracked but skill improvement is not measured.
rubric.established: Skills development progress is measured with demonstrated competency validation.
rubric.optimizing: Automated skills tracking with gamification and recognition drives continuous development engagement.

items.text: How comprehensive are the organization's IT training programs?

rubric.initial: No formal IT training programs exist.
rubric.developing: Ad-hoc training is available for some technologies.
rubric.established: Comprehensive training programs cover technical, process, and soft skills.
rubric.optimizing: Personalized learning journeys with diverse modalities address all competency areas.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use modern learning approaches (e-learning, labs)?

rubric.initial: Only traditional classroom training is available.
rubric.developing: Some e-learning content is available.
rubric.established: Blended learning with e-learning, labs, mentoring, and classroom training is standard.
rubric.optimizing: Immersive learning experiences with simulations, gamification, and adaptive content delivery.

items.text: To what extent does the organization support professional certification?

rubric.initial: No certification support exists.
rubric.developing: Certification is supported for select roles only.
rubric.established: Comprehensive certification support includes funding, study time, and exam preparation.
rubric.optimizing: Strategic certification program aligned with technology roadmap and career paths with full support.

items.text: How well does the organization measure training effectiveness?

rubric.initial: Training effectiveness is not measured.
rubric.developing: Basic completion rates are tracked.
rubric.established: Training effectiveness is measured through knowledge assessments and on-the-job application.
rubric.optimizing: ROI of training is measured with impact on performance metrics and business outcomes.

items.text: How effectively does the organization provide onboarding training for new IT staff?

rubric.initial: No structured onboarding for IT staff exists.
rubric.developing: Basic orientation is provided but technical onboarding is informal.
rubric.established: Comprehensive onboarding programs cover tools, processes, and culture for new IT staff.
rubric.optimizing: Personalized onboarding journeys with mentorship, buddy systems, and milestone-based progression.

items.text: To what degree does the organization invest in leadership development for IT managers?

rubric.initial: No IT leadership development exists.
rubric.developing: Occasional leadership workshops are offered.
rubric.established: A formal IT leadership development program includes coaching, mentoring, and rotational assignments.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive leadership pipeline development with executive coaching and cross-functional experiences.

items.text: How well does the organization foster continuous learning culture in IT?

rubric.initial: No continuous learning culture exists.
rubric.developing: Learning is seen as an event rather than ongoing.
rubric.established: Continuous learning is encouraged with allocated time and diverse resources.
rubric.optimizing: Learning is embedded in daily work with communities of practice, innovation time, and knowledge sharing.

items.text: How effectively does the organization retain IT talent?

rubric.initial: IT turnover is high with no retention strategy.
rubric.developing: Some retention efforts exist but turnover remains a concern.
rubric.established: A formal retention strategy addresses compensation, development, and work environment.
rubric.optimizing: Data-driven retention with predictive attrition modeling and personalized engagement drives low turnover.

items.text: How well does the organization offer competitive compensation for IT roles?

rubric.initial: IT compensation is not benchmarked and is below market.
rubric.developing: Some benchmarking occurs but adjustments are infrequent.
rubric.established: Regular compensation benchmarking ensures market-competitive IT salaries and benefits.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic compensation with real-time market adjustments, equity, and performance incentives.

rubric.initial: IT spending is opaque with no consolidated view.
